maven_plugin/src/main/java/io/github/cha.short_description: Enum representing Maven dependency scopes with parsing and defaulting behavior
maven_plugin/src/main/java/io/github/cha.description:
This enum centralizes Maven scope handling across the maven-lockfile plugin. It provides a typed representation of the standard Maven scopes (compile, provided, runtime, test, system, import), exposes a string value for each enum constant, and offers a parsing helper (fromString) that normalizes input and applies a default. The file exists to avoid ad-hoc string handling of scopes throughout the codebase and to make parsing, serialization, and defaulting predictable and explicit.

Design decisions: the enum stores a non-null non-empty string for each value and validates that invariant in the constructor. fromString(String) is case-insensitive, treats null or empty input as the default scope (COMPILE), and throws IllegalArgumentException for unknown values. getValue() returns the canonical string form which is likely used when serializing to the lockfile JSON or when comparing scope strings elsewhere. This enum is small but part of the public surface used by other modules that need canonical scope semantics.

key_constructs.name: MavenScope
key_constructs.purpose: Enum type representing Maven scopes and encapsulating parsing and canonical string values.
key_constructs.reasoning: Changes to this enum affect all places that interpret or serialize dependency scopes. Consumers rely on the defaulting behavior (null/empty -> COMPILE) and the case-insensitive parsing; maintain those semantics unless tests and callers are updated accordingly.

key_constructs.name: getValue
key_constructs.purpose: Returns the canonical string representation of a scope constant.
key_constructs.reasoning: Used for serialization and comparisons; any change to the returned string impacts lockfile contents and tests that assert round-trip parsing.

key_constructs.name: fromString
key_constructs.purpose: Parses an input string to a MavenScope, defaulting to COMPILE for null/empty and throwing for invalid strings.
key_constructs.reasoning: This central parser is the single source of truth for converting raw scope strings into enum values. Maintain its defaulting and error behavior because other components and tests rely on this contract.

key_behaviors: provides canonical string values for Maven scopes
parses raw scope strings into typed enum values
defaults null/empty input to COMPILE
throws on unknown scope strings

pitfalls.mistake: Altering the default behavior for null/empty inputs (currently returns COMPILE).
pitfalls.consequence: Tests (MavenScopeTest) will fail and components that implicitly rely on COMPILE defaulting may change lockfile contents and validation behavior.
pitfalls.prevention: Respect existing fromString contract; if changing, update callers and tests accordingly and understand downstream effects on lockfile generation/validation.

pitfalls.mistake: Changing getValue strings (e.g., casing or wording).
pitfalls.consequence: Serialization output (lockfile JSON) and tests expecting exact values may break; parser may no longer round-trip values.
pitfalls.prevention: When editing, keep getValue stable or provide migration/compatibility logic.

pitfalls.mistake: Removing the constructor validation that disallows null/empty values.
pitfalls.consequence: Enum constants could be created with invalid state; this would violate assumptions elsewhere about non-empty canonical scope strings and could introduce null/empty values into lockfiles.
pitfalls.prevention: Keep constructor guard or ensure alternate invariants are enforced wherever getValue is used.

fromString: parsing behavior and defaulting to COMPILE
constructor: invariant enforcement for value strings

typical enum boilerplate

Verify fromString(null) returns COMPILE (defaulting behavior).

insights.title: Introduce MavenScope enum with robust parsing and default
insights.problem: Scopes were handled as raw strings leading to potential null/empty errors and inconsistent handling.
insights.root_cause: No typed representation for Maven scopes; code had to defensively handle null/empty strings inline.
insights.solution: Introduce MavenScope enum with getValue() and fromString() which returns COMPILE if input is null or empty and throws on invalid values; add unit tests to cover parsing behavior.
insights.lesson_learned: Encapsulate parsing and defaulting behavior in a dedicated enum/class to centralize validation and to make serialization/deserialization predictable.

maven_plugin/src/test/java/io/github/cha.short_description: Unit tests for MavenScope parsing and default behavior
maven_plugin/src/test/java/io/github/cha.description:
This JUnit 5 test class verifies the parsing behavior and defaulting semantics of the MavenScope enum. It exists to lock in the expected behavior for converting raw scope strings to the typed MavenScope and for ensuring that null or empty input is treated as the default COMPILE scope. These behaviors are relied upon by other components that read or serialize dependency scopes into the generated lockfile.

The tests are small and focused: one test exercises round-trip parsing for each enum constant (calling getValue then fromString and expecting the same enum back), and two tests assert the defaulting behavior for null and empty string inputs. There is no test here for invalid inputs (expecting an exception), so that behavior is not directly exercised by the test suite.

key_constructs.name: MavenScopeTest
key_constructs.purpose: Test class containing unit tests for MavenScope functionality.
key_constructs.reasoning: Top-level test container; test runner discovers methods annotated with @Test here.

key_constructs.purpose: Verifies all enum values round-trip through getValue() -> fromString().
key_constructs.reasoning: Ensures canonical strings are parseable and parsing is case-insensitive for the defined values.

key_constructs.name: fromStringWithNull
key_constructs.purpose: Asserts that fromString(null) returns MavenScope.COMPILE.
key_constructs.reasoning: Guards the defaulting contract for null inputs used across the codebase.

key_constructs.name: fromStringWithEmpty
key_constructs.purpose: Asserts that fromString("") returns MavenScope.COMPILE.
key_constructs.reasoning: Guards the defaulting contract for empty-string inputs used across the codebase.

asserts COMPILE default for null inputs
asserts COMPILE default for empty-string inputs

pitfalls.prevention: When modifying fromString, add tests for invalid inputs or ensure callers handle exceptions as intended.

pitfalls.prevention: Use -pl maven_plugin and module-aware test invocation when running locally (see example_command).

maven_plugin/src/main/java/io/github/cha.short_description: Immutable wrapper for a version string that implements Comparable and basic validation.
maven_plugin/src/main/java/io/github/cha.description:
VersionNumber is a small value-object used to represent a version identifier as a first-class type inside the lockfile data model. It enforces non-null and non-empty input at construction time, stores the version as an immutable String, exposes the raw value via getValue(), and implements equals/hashCode and Comparable to allow using VersionNumber instances as keys, in collections, and for deterministic ordering. The class is intentionally simple: comparison is delegated to String.compareTo, and equality is a straightforward string equality check.

This file exists to provide a typed, immutable abstraction around version strings so other components (e.g., LockFile, Pom, DependencyNode) can accept a dedicated domain type rather than raw strings. Because ordering and equality are important (lockfile serialization/order stability, graph node identity), the class implements Comparable and stable equals/hashCode. The of() factory performs input validation and is the public entry point for creating instances; the constructor is private to enforce validation.

key_constructs.name: VersionNumber
key_constructs.purpose: Represents and validates an immutable version string and provides ordering and equality semantics.
key_constructs.reasoning: When modifying code that interacts with version identifiers, treat VersionNumber as the canonical typed container for version strings. Respect its validation contract (non-null, non-empty) and its lexicographical comparison semantics because other code may rely on deterministic ordering produced by compareTo.

key_behaviors: validates input is not null or empty on creation
stores version as immutable String
provides lexicographic ordering via Comparable
implements equals and hashCode based on the encapsulated string

pitfalls.mistake: Assume VersionNumber implements semantic (SemVer) comparison semantics.
pitfalls.consequence: Changing or relying on semantic version ordering when compareTo uses String.compareTo will produce mismatches in ordering and may break deterministic outputs (lockfile ordering, sorted sets).
pitfalls.prevention: When editing, preserve the current compareTo semantics unless you also update all callers and any code/tests that depend on lexicographic ordering.

pitfalls.mistake: Constructing VersionNumber with null or empty string by bypassing of().
pitfalls.consequence: Constructor is private so direct bypass is unlikely, but any attempt to change visibility or remove validation will allow invalid instances which can lead to NPEs or invalid lockfiles.
pitfalls.prevention: Keep validation (Objects.requireNonNull and Strings.isNullOrEmpty) intact and ensure factory of() remains the entry point.

pitfalls.mistake: Altering equals/hashCode or compareTo to use different normalization (trim, lower-case) without considering persistent lockfile format.
pitfalls.consequence: Will change equality and ordering semantics, potentially causing subtle diffs in lockfiles, mismatch on validation runs, or graph set membership changes.
pitfalls.prevention: Any change to equality/ordering must be coordinated with serialization, tests, and consumers expecting deterministic ordering.

reading_guide.start_here: VersionNumber.of(String) -- public factory validates input and is the intended entrypoint.
key_sections: VersionNumber.of: validates the input (non-null, non-empty) and returns the immutable object.
private constructor: assigns final value field and re-checks nulls.
compareTo: uses String.compareTo to provide ordering; important for deterministic sorts.
skip_unless_needed: toString: simple debug representation

related_files.reason_to_check: If VersionNumber comparison or equality semantics change, check Pom and any lockfile serialization or tests relying on deterministic ordering.

related_files.relationship: Dependency graph nodes often include version identifiers; ordering of nodes (compareTo usage) may rely on VersionNumber behavior.
related_files.reason_to_check: Ensure ordering, identity, and graph traversal behavior remains consistent if VersionNumber changes.

tests_to_run: mvn test (full project) to detect integration-level regressions that depend on ordering)

maven_plugin/src/main/java/io/github/cha.short_description: Immutable wrapper holding Environment and Config metadata for lockfile operations
maven_plugin/src/main/java/io/github/cha.description:
MetaData is a small value object introduced to group environment and configuration information into a single object that can be passed through the lockfile generation and validation flows. It exists to reduce parameter bloat on APIs that previously accepted separate Environment and Config parameters (commit f99517e). The class is intentionally minimal: a constructor that sets final fields and two simple getters. Its primary role is packaging two related domain objects so callers such as LockFile, LockFileFacade, GenerateLockFileMojo, and ValidateChecksumMojo can accept a single argument representing runtime metadata.

Because MetaData participates in lockfile generation and validation flows, it effectively forms part of the plugin's public model used during JSON lockfile assembly (Environment and Config data included in serialized metadata). The class opts for immutability (final fields and no setters), which signals that instances are intended to be constructed once and transported without mutation. There are no convenience methods (equals, hashCode, toString, JSON annotations) in this file, so serialization and structural expectations are implemented elsewhere (e.g., JsonUtils and LockFile model classes).

key_constructs.name: MetaData
key_constructs.purpose: Container class that holds an Environment and a Config instance together as metadata for lockfile operations
key_constructs.reasoning: Callers across the plugin previously passed Environment and Config separately; MetaData centralizes those values so API signatures are simplified and fewer changes are required when passing metadata between Mojos, LockFileFacade, and LockFile model builders. An agent editing code that constructs or consumes MetaData should preserve the constructor signature and immutability contract because callers expect a simple container object.

key_constructs.name: getConfig
key_constructs.purpose: Accessor returning the Config instance stored in this MetaData
key_constructs.reasoning: Used by downstream consumers to read configuration options that influence lockfile generation and validation. Any edits must preserve the getter name and return type to avoid breaking call sites or serialization mapping.

callers.context: Config config = lockFileFromFile.getConfig() == null ? getConfig() : lockFileFromFile.getConfig();

key_constructs.name: getEnvironment
key_constructs.purpose: Accessor returning the Environment instance stored in this MetaData
key_constructs.reasoning: Used by downstream consumers to access environment information (CI environment, platform info, etc.). Keep the method signature intact when refactoring to avoid breaking integration with other components that expect this accessor.

key_behaviors: groups Environment and Config into a single object for API calls
provides read-only access to environment and configuration via getters
serves as metadata payload carried through lockfile generation and validation

pitfalls.mistake: Removing or renaming constructor parameters or getters
pitfalls.consequence: Breaks all callers that construct MetaData or call getEnvironment/getConfig (compilation errors and runtime issues when assembling lockfile metadata).
pitfalls.prevention: Preserve the constructor signature and public getter method names, or update all call sites and any serialization code in one coordinated change.

pitfalls.mistake: Adding mutability (setters or non-final fields)
pitfalls.consequence: Could allow accidental modification of metadata mid-flow, violating assumptions about immutability and making debugging harder for lockfile reproducibility or validation comparisons.
pitfalls.prevention: Maintain final fields and provide only read access, or ensure any new mutability is documented and all consumers updated accordingly.

pitfalls.mistake: Assuming JSON serialization or equality behaviour is handled here
pitfalls.consequence: Adding equals/hashCode/toString or JSON annotations without checking how JsonUtils and LockFile serialization expect the structure could change serialized output or break consumers relying on exact JSON layout.
pitfalls.prevention: If modifications affect serialization, verify interactions with JsonUtils and LockFile model, and run full serialization tests (mvn test) and any lockfile generation flows in CI.

related_files.reason_to_check: Any change to MetaData's shape or getters must be synchronized with how LockFileFacade reads environment/config values; serialization and consumers that build lockfile metadata depend on these fields.

related_files.reason_to_check: If the Mojo's construction of MetaData changes (different Environment/Config sources), the locking behavior and CLI flags may be impacted.

related_files.relationship: Validation flow consumes metadata to decide validation modes and allow-failure settings; MetaData must continue to supply expected values.
related_files.reason_to_check: Validation logic relies on metadata for behavior; ensure getter availability and semantics remain stable.

related_files.reason_to_check: Lockfile JSON layout and how metadata fields are serialized/consumed must remain consistent with changes to MetaData.

related_files.relationship: Responsible for serialization/deserialization of models including metadata; changes to MetaData could affect JSON handling.
related_files.reason_to_check: If MetaData's structure changes, check JsonUtils behavior and unit/integration tests that serialize/deserialize lockfiles.

insights.title: Introduce MetaData wrapper for Environment + Config
insights.problem: Metadata about environment and config were represented separately in different places; easier to pass as single object.
insights.root_cause: Previous Metadata (renamed Environment) and Config scatter made method signatures messy.
insights.solution: Add MetaData class holding Environment and Config; update LockFile, GenerateLockFileMojo, ValidateChecksumMojo and LockFileFacade to accept MetaData.
insights.lesson_learned: Group related metadata into a single model object to simplify method signatures and reduce parameter mismatches.

maven_plugin/src/main/java/io/github/cha.short_description: Immutable value object representing a dependency node identifier (group:artifact:version).
maven_plugin/src/main/java/io/github/cha.description:
NodeId is a small, focused value object that models the canonical identity of an artifact node in the project's dependency graph. It bundles three domain value types (GroupId, ArtifactId, VersionNumber) and provides stable equals/hashCode semantics and a single-string representation in the conventional Maven form "group:artifact:version". The class exists to centralize identity handling for graph nodes and to make identity comparisons and text serialization/deserialization consistent across the lockfile generator and validator.

Design notes: NodeId is immutable (final fields) and exposes a package-private constructor, indicating intent to control creation to code inside the graph package while still offering a public factory fromValue(String). toString() and fromValue(String) form a basic serialization pair using ':' as a delimiter. hashCode/equals are implemented via Objects.hash/equals on the three wrapped value objects. Because NodeId wraps GroupId/ArtifactId/VersionNumber objects, it delegates string extraction to their getValue() methods and relies on their own invariants (e.g., non-null, validated content).

key_constructs.name: NodeId
key_constructs.purpose: Represents the identity of an artifact node as a composite of groupId, artifactId, and version.
key_constructs.reasoning: This class centralizes identity logic used when building or comparing the dependency graph. Its immutability and equals/hashCode contract make it suitable as a map/set key or graph node identifier. The package-private constructor forces creation to be controlled within the package, while fromValue(String) provides a public parser for textual representations.

key_behaviors: creates NodeId instances from typed components
parses colon-separated identity strings into NodeId
serializes NodeId into the conventional "group:artifact:version" string
provides stable equals/hashCode for use in maps/sets/graphs

pitfalls.mistake: Assuming fromValue(String) is resilient to malformed input (e.g., missing parts or extra colons).
pitfalls.consequence: A malformed string can cause ArrayIndexOutOfBoundsException or pass invalid components to GroupId/ArtifactId/VersionNumber (which may throw). This can crash lockfile generation/validation.
pitfalls.prevention: Ensure callers validate or catch exceptions around fromValue; respect that fromValue splits on ':' and expects exactly three segments.

pitfalls.mistake: Changing the delimiter in toString()/fromValue() or their formatting without coordinating consumers.
pitfalls.consequence: Breaks compatibility with persisted lockfiles, maps, or other code that relies on the "group:artifact:version" format.
pitfalls.prevention: Treat the current string format as a stable serialization contract; if changes are necessary, update all consumers and serialization/deserialization entrypoints together.

pitfalls.mistake: Assuming NodeId constructor is public and freely instantiable from any package.
pitfalls.consequence: Refactors that attempt to instantiate NodeId from outside io.github.chains_project.maven_lockfile.graph may fail to compile because the constructor is package-private.
pitfalls.prevention: Respect the package-private constructor; use fromValue or add a new public factory if broader construction is required, and check callers accordingly.

pitfalls.mistake: Treating NodeId as mutable or expecting defensive copies of inner value objects.
pitfalls.consequence: If underlying GroupId/ArtifactId/VersionNumber implementations were changed to be mutable, NodeId immutability guarantee could be broken.
pitfalls.prevention: Assume immutability of the wrapped types and preserve that contract when editing related classes.

maven_plugin/src/main/java/io/github/cha.short_description: Represents structured differences between two lockfiles (deps and plugins).
maven_plugin/src/main/java/io/github/cha.description:
LockFileDifference is a small reporting/data-holder class whose purpose is to capture the structured differences between a lockfile read from disk and a lockfile generated from the current project. It exists to provide a clear, typed representation of what is missing in either the file or the project across two domains: dependencies (DependencyNode) and Maven plugins (MavenPlugin). The class centralizes the diff computation and exposes defensive getters to callers that will build user-facing diagnostics or fail a validation build (e.g., ValidateChecksumMojo).

The primary design choice is immutability of the exposed API: internal sets are stored in private final fields and each getter returns a new HashSet copy (defensive copying) to avoid exposing internal mutable state. The static factory method diff(...) computes differences using Guava's Sets.difference between concrete HashSet copies of the LockFile-provided collections and stores those results. Consumers are expected to iterate the returned copies to present messages or to decide on build failure. This class purposely keeps no logic beyond set-difference computation and copying — it is a focused utility used by higher-level validation/reporting code in the plugin.

key_constructs.name: LockFileDifference
key_constructs.purpose: Container for differences between two LockFile instances (missing deps/plugins in both directions).
key_constructs.reasoning: Acts as a structured result returned by diff(...). An agent editing code that consumes or produces diffs must respect this class's role as the canonical representation of lockfile vs project mismatches.

key_constructs.name: diff
key_constructs.purpose: Static factory that computes the difference between two LockFile objects and returns a LockFileDifference.
key_constructs.reasoning: This is the core entry point: it converts lockfile collections into HashSets, computes set differences via Guava, and packages them. Changes to inputs or result representation should preserve the semantics of 'missing in project' vs 'missing in file'.

key_constructs.name: getMissingDependenciesInFile
key_constructs.purpose: Returns a defensive copy of dependencies present in the project-generated lockfile but missing in the on-disk file.
key_constructs.reasoning: Guarantees callers cannot mutate internal state. Any consumer expecting to modify the returned Set must be aware changes won't reflect back into the LockFileDifference instance.

key_constructs.name: getMissingDependenciesInProject
key_constructs.purpose: Returns a defensive copy of dependencies present in the on-disk file but missing in the project-generated lockfile.
key_constructs.reasoning: Same defensive-copy contract as other getters; preserves immutability of the held sets.

key_constructs.name: getMissingPluginsInFile
key_constructs.purpose: Returns a defensive copy of plugins present in the project-generated lockfile but missing in the on-disk file.
key_constructs.reasoning: Plugins are treated separately from dependencies; equality/hash semantics of MavenPlugin are relied upon for correct set-difference results.

key_constructs.name: getMissingPluginsInProject
key_constructs.purpose: Returns a defensive copy of plugins present in the on-disk file but missing in the project-generated lockfile.
key_constructs.reasoning: Used by validation/reporting code paths to construct informative messages about plugin mismatches.

key_behaviors: computes set differences between two LockFile objects
exposes missing dependencies in file vs project
exposes missing plugins in file vs project
returns defensive copies of internal sets

pitfalls.mistake: Treating the Sets.difference result as an immutable snapshot rather than a Guava view.
pitfalls.consequence: Because Guava's Sets.difference returns a live view, if the backing sets were mutated after creation the view could change. Here the backing sets are local HashSet copies, so this is safe; but refactoring to pass through original collections could introduce surprising mutations.
pitfalls.prevention: When modifying diff(...), ensure that the inputs are materialized (as done now with new HashSet<>(...)) before creating the difference view, or convert the view to a concrete collection if persistence is required.

pitfalls.mistake: Assuming equality/hashCode implementations for DependencyNode and MavenPlugin are consistent with identity semantics used here.
pitfalls.consequence: If equals/hashCode for DependencyNode or MavenPlugin are incorrect or change, set difference results will be wrong, leading to false positives/negatives in validation errors.
pitfalls.prevention: When changing representations of DependencyNode or MavenPlugin, verify their equals/hashCode semantics and update tests consuming LockFileDifference.

pitfalls.mistake: Returning internal mutable collections directly from getters.
pitfalls.consequence: External mutation of internal state would break immutability guarantees and could cause inconsistent reports.
pitfalls.prevention: Respect existing defensive-copy pattern: getters must continue to return new HashSet<>(internalSet).

key_sections: diff: computes the missings using Guava Sets.difference and constructs the object
getMissingDependenciesInFile: defensive getter semantics (applies also to other getters)
skip_unless_needed: constructor: trivial private constructor that stores fields (no complex logic)

related_files.relationship: Source of the LockFile instances that diff(...) compares; contains dependency and plugin collections used as inputs.
related_files.reason_to_check: Changes to LockFile's collection types or element equality semantics will affect Set difference outcomes and must be validated.

related_files.relationship: Element type used in dependency sets; its equals/hashCode control set membership and diff results.
related_files.reason_to_check: Any change to DependencyNode's identity/equals/hashCode will change diff behavior and reporting output.

related_files.relationship: Element type used in plugin sets; its equality controls plugin diff results.
related_files.reason_to_check: Mutating MavenPlugin's equality semantics or fields used in comparisons will alter what is reported as missing.

related_files.relationship: Consumer of LockFileDifference for building user-facing validation errors and determining build failure.
related_files.reason_to_check: Alterations to the shape or contract of LockFileDifference (e.g., changing returned types) will require updating ValidateChecksumMojo's usage.

insights.title: Return copies of internal sets for safer reporting API
insights.problem: LockFileDifference getters returned internal mutable sets directly.
insights.root_cause: Returning internal mutable collections exposes internal state.
insights.solution: Return new HashSet<>(internalSet) from getters to return defensive copies.
insights.lesson_learned: API getters should return immutable or defensive copies to avoid accidental mutation of internal state.

insights.title: Structured diff representation for lockfile mismatches
insights.problem: Previous diffs were ad-hoc and didn't clearly separate missing dependencies vs plugins.
insights.root_cause: No central representation for differences between a lockfile on disk and generated lockfile.
insights.solution: Added LockFileDifference.diff that computes missing dependencies and missing plugins in both directions and exposes getters used in ValidateChecksumMojo to build verbose error messages.
insights.lesson_learned: Provide a structured diff object when comparing artifacts to improve diagnostics and avoid string parsing of logs.

maven_plugin/src/main/java/io/github/cha.short_description: Gson TypeAdapterFactory that serializes empty lists as JSON null to work around a Gson bug.
maven_plugin/src/main/java/io/github/cha.description:
This class implements com.google.gson.TypeAdapterFactory and provides a special TypeAdapter for List types that writes empty Java Lists as JSON null instead of an empty JSON array. It exists as a targeted workaround for a known Gson behavior/bug (referenced in the inline comment as https://github.com/google/gson/issues/1028). The factory distinguishes whether it was constructed by Gson via @JsonAdapter (deprecated no-arg ctor) versus programmatically (INSTANCE) and chooses whether to obtain the delegate adapter via gson.getAdapter(type) or gson.getDelegateAdapter(this, type). This distinction affects how delegate adapters are resolved and preserves any special behavior the delegate might have for nulls. The factory leaves the read path to the delegate unchanged and only alters the write(path) behavior: when writing, if the list is null or empty it delegates writing a null to the delegate adapter; otherwise, it delegates the actual list writing.

It is intended to be registered with Gson so that when serializing domain objects that contain List fields the choice to output null for empty lists is applied consistently. In the project, data model classes that are serialized into the lockfile JSON (LockFile, Pom, MavenPlugin, etc.) may be influenced by this behavior if the Gson instance used to write the lockfile registers this factory (check JsonUtils or Gson configuration). The class is small and focused, designed with minimal state: a single boolean wasCreatedByJsonAdapter and a public static INSTANCE for typical registration.

Design choices preserved here: (1) explicit handling only for List subtypes, (2) delegation to the underlying TypeAdapter for reading and writing except for the special-case write when value is empty or null, and (3) careful use of gson.getAdapter vs gson.getDelegateAdapter conditional on how this factory was created, which matches Gson's expectations for @JsonAdapter-created factories. This code depends on correct casting of generic TypeAdapter objects and uses an anonymous TypeAdapter<List<Object>> implementation to bridge type erasure.

key_constructs.name: EmptyListToNullFactory
key_constructs.purpose: Gson TypeAdapterFactory that returns a TypeAdapter for List types which writes empty lists as JSON null.
key_constructs.reasoning: Readers or writers of lockfile JSON sometimes must represent empty collections as nulls to match consumers or to avoid a specific Gson bug/behavior; encapsulating this logic in a TypeAdapterFactory allows centralized registration with a Gson instance and keeps data classes untouched.

key_constructs.name: INSTANCE
key_constructs.purpose: Public singleton instance of EmptyListToNullFactory created with wasCreatedByJsonAdapter=false for programmatic registration.
key_constructs.reasoning: Most code will register a single shared instance with Gson; using INSTANCE preserves consistent behavior and avoids accidentally constructing the factory with the JsonAdapter-specific constructor semantics.

key_constructs.name: create
key_constructs.purpose: Implementation of TypeAdapterFactory#create that returns a List-specific TypeAdapter which delegates reads and conditionally writes nulls for empty lists.
key_constructs.reasoning: This is the core behavior provider: it checks whether the requested type is a List; if so returns an adapter that delegates reading and handles writing empty lists by delegating a null write to the delegate adapter.

key_behaviors: returns a Gson TypeAdapter for List types
reads lists using the delegate adapter without changes
writes null via delegate when a List is null or empty
resolves delegate adapter using gson.getAdapter or gson.getDelegateAdapter depending on construction mode

pitfalls.mistake: Removing or changing the wasCreatedByJsonAdapter behavior and unconditionally calling gson.getDelegateAdapter(this, type) or gson.getAdapter(type).
pitfalls.consequence: May change how delegate adapters are resolved when the factory is instantiated by Gson via @JsonAdapter, potentially breaking custom null handling or adapter selection done by Gson for annotated types.
pitfalls.prevention: Preserve the conditional resolution logic and the deprecated constructor semantics; maintain the distinction between programmatic registration and JsonAdapter-created factories.

pitfalls.mistake: Casting the delegate TypeAdapter to TypeAdapter<List<Object>> without respecting the actual parameterized element type.
pitfalls.consequence: Generics are erased at runtime; incorrect assumptions could lead to ClassCastException or incorrect serialization for lists of specific element types if further changes introduce type-sensitive behavior.
pitfalls.prevention: Do not alter the unchecked casts without adjusting how the delegate is obtained; keep the current suppression and ensure any changed code still handles raw List<Object> safely.

pitfalls.mistake: Changing the write behavior to directly write JsonWriter.nullValue() instead of delegating delegate.write(out, null).
pitfalls.consequence: If the delegate has special null handling (e.g., custom adapter that produces something other than plain JSON null), that behavior will be lost. Tests relying on delegate null handling may break.
pitfalls.prevention: Keep delegate.write(out, null) to preserve delegate-specific null semantics as the code currently does.

key_sections: create: determines List-ness, obtains delegate adapter correctly, and constructs the anonymous TypeAdapter that overrides write to convert empty lists to null via delegate
private EmptyListToNullFactory() (deprecated): explains the JsonAdapter-construction path and why wasCreatedByJsonAdapter flag exists
INSTANCE constant: typical programmatic registration site
skip_unless_needed: the small anonymous TypeAdapter read method body (it delegates to the delegate adapter and contains no special behavior beyond delegation)

related_files.relationship: Likely registers or configures Gson instances used to serialize lockfile data; this factory would be registered there to affect lockfile output.
related_files.reason_to_check: Any change to adapter behavior should be validated against Gson configuration and where Gson instances are created (JsonUtils) to ensure the factory is registered and used as intended.

related_files.relationship: LockFile and other data classes are serialized to JSON for the lockfile; their List fields are the targets of this factory's behavior.
related_files.reason_to_check: Changes here could alter how empty lists in the lockfile are emitted (null vs []), so ensure any edits remain compatible with consumers that parse the lockfile.

maven_plugin/src/test/java/io/github/cha.short_description: Unit test asserting LockFile equality is order-insensitive and building sample dependency/plugin fixtures.
maven_plugin/src/test/java/io/github/cha.description:
This JUnit 5 test class verifies equality semantics for the LockFile data model. The core scenario constructs two LockFile instances that contain the same logical data but with different insertion orders for dependency roots and plugins; the test asserts these two instances are equal. The file exists to enforce that the serialized/in-memory lockfile model treats collections of dependencies, plugins and BOMs in an order-insensitive way (Sets) so that logically equivalent lockfiles do not fail equality checks due to ordering differences.

The test also doubles as a set of fixtures demonstrating how to construct DependencyNode instances (including parent->child relationships), MavenPlugin instances, Pom/BOM entries, and MetaData/Config payloads needed to instantiate a LockFile. It ensures that the test fixtures use newer model elements added during model evolution (Pom in LockFile; ResolvedUrl.Unresolved(); RepositoryId.None(); ChecksumModes enum; Config enum values). Because the test relies on the implementations of equals/hashCode across LockFile and nested types (DependencyNode, MavenPlugin, Pom), it functions as a contract ensuring those implementations remain order-insensitive and stable across refactors.

key_constructs.name: LockfileTest
key_constructs.purpose: Container for unit tests that assert LockFile equality semantics and provides fixture builders.
key_constructs.reasoning: This class centralizes the scenario that verifies order-insensitive equality for LockFile instances and provides helper methods used to build the objects under test; when editing related models, this test must be kept in sync with constructor signatures and enum changes.

key_constructs.name: shouldLockFilesEqualWhenOrderIsChanged
key_constructs.purpose: JUnit test that creates two LockFile instances with identical logical content but different Set insertion orders and asserts equality.
key_constructs.reasoning: This is the primary assertion that the LockFile model and its nested members compare equal regardless of ordering; any change to the equality/hashCode behavior of included types will affect this test.

key_constructs.name: dependencyNodeA
key_constructs.purpose: Helper that constructs a DependencyNode 'A' with two child nodes added in sequence.
key_constructs.reasoning: Used to build a small subgraph demonstrating parent-child relationships. The addition order of children is part of the fixture; tests rely on equality semantics that should ignore child insertion order if the overall model intends to be order-insensitive.

key_constructs.name: dependencyNodeB
key_constructs.purpose: Helper that constructs a single leaf DependencyNode 'B'.
key_constructs.reasoning: Provides a second root node to test Set ordering between roots.

key_constructs.name: dependencyNodeAChild1
key_constructs.purpose: Helper that constructs a child DependencyNode 'A1'.
key_constructs.reasoning: Used as a child for dependencyNodeA and to ensure children with different artifact types are represented in equality checks.

key_constructs.name: dependencyNodeAChild2
key_constructs.purpose: Helper that constructs a child DependencyNode 'A2'.
key_constructs.reasoning: Paired with dependencyNodeAChild1 to build a multi-child scenario.

key_constructs.name: pluginA
key_constructs.purpose: Helper that constructs a MavenPlugin fixture 'PA'.
key_constructs.reasoning: Used to assert that the plugins Set ordering does not affect LockFile equality; uses ResolvedUrl.Unresolved() and RepositoryId.None() to match model expectations.

key_constructs.name: pluginB
key_constructs.purpose: Helper that constructs a MavenPlugin fixture 'PB'.
key_constructs.reasoning: Paired with pluginA to provide multiple plugin entries and test order-insensitivity.

key_constructs.name: bom
key_constructs.purpose: Helper that constructs a Pom object representing a BOM entry for inclusion in LockFile.boms.
key_constructs.reasoning: Ensures that BOM entries are part of the LockFile equality comparison and uses Pom constructor shape added in model evolution.

key_behaviors: constructs sample dependency graph nodes with children
constructs sample MavenPlugin and Pom/BOM fixtures
asserts LockFile equality is independent of insertion order into Sets
uses model conveniences like ResolvedUrl.Unresolved() and RepositoryId.None()

pitfalls.mistake: Assuming equality of LockFile and nested types is based on insertion order (e.g., lists) rather than on set semantics.
pitfalls.consequence: Tests will start failing intermittently or deterministically after refactors if equals/hashCode semantics change to be order-sensitive.
pitfalls.prevention: When changing model classes (LockFile, DependencyNode, MavenPlugin, Pom), ensure equals/hashCode are maintained or update this test to reflect intended semantics.

pitfalls.mistake: Changing constructors or required fields (Pom signature, RepositoryId, ResolvedUrl, Config enums) without updating tests.
pitfalls.consequence: Compilation failures in this test or runtime NPEs when fixtures no longer satisfy new invariants.
pitfalls.prevention: Keep test fixtures in sync with model evolution; use the test as a contract for constructor/API signatures.

pitfalls.mistake: Altering DependencyNode.addChild semantics (e.g., making it mutable in a way that affects equals/hashCode).
pitfalls.consequence: Equality checks could become dependent on child insertion order or mutable state, causing flakiness.
pitfalls.prevention: Respect immutability or consistent ordering guarantees in graph children or reflect such changes in the test expectations.

key_sections: shouldLockFilesEqualWhenOrderIsChanged: constructs two LockFile instances with different insertion orders and asserts equality
dependencyNodeA/dependencyNodeB/dependencyNodeAChild1/dependencyNodeAChild2: helper fixtures building a small dependency graph
pluginA/pluginB and bom: helper fixtures for plugin and BOM entries
skip_unless_needed: import block
minor helper methods if you only care about the main test assertion

relevant_snippets.description: Constructs metadata, two LockFile instances with different ordering for dependencies and plugins, and asserts equality.

related_files.relationship: Defines the LockFile model under test; equality/hashCode implementations here determine test behavior.
related_files.reason_to_check: If LockFile fields or equality semantics change, update tests and fixtures accordingly.

related_files.relationship: Defines DependencyNode used by test fixtures and whose child handling influences equality.
related_files.reason_to_check: Changes to child storage, addChild behavior, or equals/hashCode will affect this test.

related_files.relationship: Pom is used for the root POM and BOM entry in the test; constructor or field changes must be reflected in fixtures.
related_files.reason_to_check: Model evolution has previously required test updates (see commit insights).

related_files.relationship: MavenPlugin fixtures are used to test plugins Set ordering and equality.
related_files.reason_to_check: Changing plugin fields or equals/hashCode will alter test outcomes.

related_files.relationship: ChecksumModes enum is referenced in the test metadata; enum changes could break test compilation.
related_files.reason_to_check: If enum entries are removed/renamed, update test usage.

tests_to_run: mvn test (to run full test suite)

insights.title: Unit tests updated to reflect new data model (pom, resolved url, repository id, enums)
insights.problem: Lockfile unit tests needed updates after changes to Config enums, Pom addition to LockFile, ResolvedUrl/RepositoryId in nodes and plugins.
insights.root_cause: Model evolution changed constructors and expected serialized content.
insights.solution: Adapt test fixtures to construct Pom, supply ResolvedUrl.Unresolved() and RepositoryId.None(), use ChecksumModes enum etc.
insights.lesson_learned: When evolving data models, update unit tests simultaneously; they serve as a contract for serialized structure.

insights.title: Unit test asserts order-insensitive equality for LockFile with Sets
insights.problem: Previously tests assumed List ordering; after refactor to Set, tests needed to assert equality independent of insertion order.
insights.root_cause: Refactor to use Set for dependencies/plugins required test updates to validate equality under different insertion orders.
insights.solution: New LockfileTest constructs LockFile instances with Sets in different order and asserts they are equal.
insights.lesson_learned: When changing collection semantics in models, add unit tests to assert the intended equality and ordering invariants.

insights.title: Document command-line flags and -D property usage in README
insights.problem: Users were unclear how to set plugin flags from the command line; docs lacked -D examples.
insights.root_cause: README earlier listed flags but not the exact -D property forms or examples of usage.
insights.solution: Add explicit mapping of flags to -D properties and a concrete mvn invocation example (e.g. -DincludeMavenPlugins=true -DlockfileName=my-lockfile.json).
insights.lesson_learned: Documentation should show exact CLI forms and examples for configuration flags, especially for Maven -D properties used in CI.

insights.title: Make README reference concrete plugin coordinates via templating
insights.problem: README previously contained ${CurrentVersion} placeholder that was not automatically updated, causing confusion for users running example commands.
insights.root_cause: Docs were not generated from project metadata during CI/release, requiring manual updates.
insights.solution: Switched README examples to use the generated filtered content (project.version replaced into README) via mvn resources:copy-resources.
insights.lesson_learned: Keep usage examples in docs tied to project.version via templating so they stay accurate across releases.

insights.title: Minor grammar fix
insights.problem: Small grammar mistake in README.
insights.root_cause: Typo.
insights.solution: Fix wording in README.
insights.lesson_learned: Docs clarity helps onboarding but doesn't affect runtime behaviour.

insights.title: Rebrand documentation to maven-lockfile
insights.problem: Documentation referenced the old plugin name/artifact coordinates 'integrity-maven-plugin', which no longer matches the codebase.
insights.root_cause: Renaming the plugin artifact required documentation updates.
insights.solution: Update badges, examples and search links to reference maven-lockfile coordinates.
insights.lesson_learned: Documentation must be updated as part of rebranding; consider search-and-replace tooling and verify examples (mvn command strings) match the packaged artifacts.

insights.title: Add contents: write permission and move to mvn plugin invocation
insights.problem: Release workflow needed permission to write back lockfile changes (commit updated lockfile) and older steps used jbang to run the action which had indent/format issues.
insights.root_cause: GitHub Actions requires explicit contents: write permission when committing changes from a workflow using a token; earlier action invocation using jbang/CLI was brittle.
insights.solution: Add permissions.contents: write to job, change to running mvn io.github.chains-project:maven-lockfile:generate directly in several steps and remove jbang usage in release flow.
insights.lesson_learned: Workflows that commit changes must request write permission explicitly; prefer invoking plugin directly in release flows instead of external jbang wrappers to reduce fragility.

insights.title: Fix conditional expression and ensure maven-lockfile runs during release
insights.problem: Conditional logic for semver next sometimes evaluated incorrectly; docs generation and lockfile execution steps were missing or inconsistent in the release flow.
insights.root_cause: Malformed or spaced conditional expressions in workflow and earlier absence of a stable doc-generation step replaced sed usage.
insights.solution: Fixed conditional (combined OR expression) formatting, added mvn resources:copy-resources steps to generate README/action.yml, and added runs of maven-lockfile in release flow where needed.
insights.lesson_learned: CI workflow boolean/if syntax is sensitive; test workflows end-to-end and prefer explicit doc generation steps rather than ad-hoc shell substitutions. Ensure lockfile generation occurs in release flows to keep docs and action coordinates updated.

insights.title: Adjust Java runtime version used by release workflow
insights.problem: The JReleaser workflow requested Java 17 in some commits and Java 11 in others; misaligned Java version can break tool compatibility.
insights.root_cause: Different commits toggled java-version between 11 and 17 to match upstream tooling requirements.
insights.solution: Updated actions/setup-java java-version field appropriately (commits show toggles between 11 and 17) to align with tooling requirements.
insights.lesson_learned: Ensure CI Java version matches tools used (JReleaser, plugin requirements); prefer documenting required Java versions and pinning them in workflows.

insights.title: Handle semver.next with SNAPSHOT correctly for major/minor increments
insights.problem: Releasing from a SNAPSHOT version using semver next produced incorrect NEXT_VERSION for major/minor bumps.
insights.root_cause: semver next against a SNAPSHOT (X.Y.Z-SNAPSHOT) will resolve to X.Y.Z (the same patch), so a single semver next wasn't enough for major/minor bumps—the script needed to run semver next twice to get the intended next major/minor version.
insights.solution: Updated workflow logic to run semver next twice for major/minor version increments; added conditional steps and comments explaining the behavior; also added a Set up Maven step.
insights.lesson_learned: Tools that operate on semantic versions may behave differently with '-SNAPSHOT' qualifiers—scripts must account for that. When modifying release automation, test edge cases for patch/minor/major flows.
constructs: Set next version (patch/major/minor) double semver run

insights.title: Improve release script logging and flow
insights.problem: Release workflow lacked clear comments and had some steps ordered suboptimally (version calculation, sed replacements).
insights.root_cause: Initial release steps were terse and did not handle snapshot -> release -> post-release snapshot transitions clearly.
insights.solution: Added explanatory comments, reorganized NEXT_VERSION setting, updated sed replacements, and added staging and changelog appending steps clarity.
insights.lesson_learned: Complex release workflows benefit from explicit comments and clear separation of stages (determine release version, stage, publish, update changelog, bump to next snapshot).

insights.title: Use NEXT_VERSION_WITHOUT_SNAPSHOT in sed replacements for Java files
insights.problem: Release workflow used NEXT_VERSION (with -SNAPSHOT) when replacing coordinates inside Java files, which could result in incorrect references (including -SNAPSHOT) in source files or action metadata.
insights.root_cause: The sed replacement used the NEXT_VERSION variable rather than the snapshot-stripped NEXT_VERSION_WITHOUT_SNAPSHOT for Java source coordinates.
insights.solution: Change the sed replacement to use env.NEXT_VERSION_WITHOUT_SNAPSHOT for Java files while keeping action.yml replacements with NEXT_VERSION where appropriate.
insights.lesson_learned: Be careful when performing automated text replacements in source files during releases; ensure you use the appropriate version form (with/without -SNAPSHOT) depending on target context.

insights.title: Ensure sed applies also to action.yml and use multiline run for complex commands
insights.problem: Earlier workflow had a one-line sed that missed replacing occurrences in action.yml or needed proper quoting across lines.
insights.root_cause: The run step used a one-liner and didn't include all relevant files.
insights.solution: Wrap sed invocations in a multiline run block and include action.yml in the replacements.
insights.lesson_learned: Complex release automation scripts are fragile; test sed/replace operations locally and constrain replacements to intended files.

insights.title: Fix semver/semversion usage and NEXT_VERSION handling in release workflow
insights.problem: Previous workflow used incorrect semversion command invocation and inconsistent NEXT_VERSION handling which produced wrong release versions or incorrect env propagation into later steps.
insights.root_cause: Misuse of the semver/semversion CLI API (semver increment vs semver next) and inconsistent quoting of environment variables. Also versions:set wasn't run with processAllModules when needed.
insights.solution: Switch to semver next, compute CURRENT_VERSION via mvn help:evaluate, compute NEXT_VERSION correctly, use -DprocessAllModules on versions:set where appropriate, and export the correct env variables into JReleaser step. Also add a step to print/verify next version.
insights.lesson_learned: Release scripts are brittle: explicitly derive versions via mvn help:evaluate and use the right semver CLI syntax. For multi-module projects use -DprocessAllModules. Check workflow shell quoting carefully.

insights.title: Attempt to patch Java sources with new artifact coordinates during release
insights.problem: A sed find-and-replace step was added in the release workflow to replace old artifact coordinates inside Java files with the new version. The inserted command appears to have an extra trailing backtick which will break the shell step.
insights.root_cause: Ad-hoc approach to update hard-coded strings in Java files inside the release workflow and a syntax / quoting mistake in the run command.
insights.solution: Avoid applying sed replacements in CI unless properly validated; if needed, use safer scripting or update sources in the repo prior to release. Remove trailing stray backtick and verify quoting.
insights.lesson_learned: Automated source replacements in CI are error-prone. Use unit tests or a dry-run stage for release scripts and ensure shell quoting is correct.

insights.title: Switch to semversion choice release action and compute next version automatically
insights.problem: Previous manual input for release/nextVersion led to mistakes and inconsistent versioning.
insights.root_cause: Manual version input without semver helper increased chance of human error.
insights.solution: Changed workflow to accept 'version' as a semver choice (major/minor/patch), installed semver tool via Go, computed CURRENT_VERSION from pom (stripping -SNAPSHOT), incremented semantic version (semver increment), and used NEXT_VERSION for versions:set and commit messages. Also fixed tag pushing behavior by removing 'tags: true' where inappropriate.
insights.lesson_learned: Automate semantic version calculation in release pipelines to reduce human error; ensure commits that set SNAPSHOT for next version use appropriate token and flags to avoid tag pushing issues.

insights.title: Fix git tag pushing by removing tags flag on push step
insights.problem: Pushing tags unintentionally or failing due to incorrect push flags in Action step.
insights.root_cause: actions-js/push@master usage included tags: true in the step for SNAPSHOT commit which could cause unwanted tag pushes or push failures with restricted tokens.
insights.solution: Removed tags: true from the push step.
insights.lesson_learned: Careful with push semantics from GitHub Actions; avoid pushing tags unless explicitly desired and ensure token has permissions.

insights.title: Update GitHub Action version reference to v5.3.5 / v5.3.6-SNAPSHOT in workflows
insights.problem: Workflows referred to older pinned action SHAs; upgrades needed to pick up fixes/features.
insights.root_cause: Release bump and snapshot updates changed action artifact versions referenced in CI files.
insights.solution: Update uses: chains-project/maven-lockfile@<sha> to newer commit SHAs matching releases (v5.3.5) and bump internal pom versions to SNAPSHOT in release pipeline.
insights.lesson_learned: Keep workflow action references aligned with project releases and snapshots. When bumping versions, ensure lockfile and pom metadata are updated atomically.

insights.title: Add workflow for running the maven-lockfile action on PRs
insights.problem: No dedicated workflow existed to run the lockfile action on pull requests by default for repository validation.
insights.root_cause: Consumers expected a sample Lockfile workflow; repo lacked a recommended workflow for typical usage.
insights.solution: Added .github/workflows/Lockfile.yml to call uses: chains-project/maven-lockfile@v1.1.7 on pull_request.
insights.lesson_learned: Provide a canonical example workflow in the repo to improve discoverability and adoption; ensure it uses recommended inputs (github-token).

insights.title: Add a workflow to regenerate lockfile on demand
insights.problem: No easy manual trigger to regenerate lockfiles in CI existed.
insights.root_cause: Need for a manual workflow to regenerate lockfiles (workflow_dispatch).
insights.solution: Add regenerate-lockfile.yml with workflow_dispatch trigger and a job that runs maven-lockfile action.
insights.lesson_learned: Provide a manual dispatch CI workflow for regenerating artifacts like lockfiles to aid maintainers.

insights.title: Provide fixture lockfile for freeze test
insights.problem: Integration test needs a deterministic lockfile to drive freeze mojo behavior.
insights.root_cause: Freeze mojo reads lockfile from project path; tests must include a sample lockfile.
insights.solution: Added lockfile.json fixture with junit-jupiter-api dependency tree and checksums.
insights.lesson_learned: Include realistic lockfile fixtures when testing lockfile-driven behavior; ensure metadata fields exist so code paths comparing metadata are exercised.

insights.title: Add reproducibility check job to CI
insights.problem: Need to verify build reproducibility for the maven plugin across runs.
insights.root_cause: Reproducibility was not explicitly checked in CI.
insights.solution: Added reproducilbilty job that builds twice (with --update-snapshots then clean install) to check consistent results.
insights.lesson_learned: CI should include a reproducibility check for build artifacts in projects focused on reproducible builds; ensure job working-directory is set to the maven_plugin submodule.

insights.title: Add pom quality check and ensure Maven is set up for the job
insights.problem: CI job didn't explicitly set up Maven or run a central pom-quality check step.
insights.root_cause: The code-quality workflow omitted setup steps required for consistent Maven runs and did not execute pomchecker.
insights.solution: Added stCarolas/setup-maven step and a Check maven pom quality run that executes pomchecker-maven-plugin to validate POM quality for Maven Central.
insights.lesson_learned: Include environment setup (Maven) in CI jobs that run maven goals; use pomchecker to catch Maven Central policy issues early.

insights.title: Add Maven setup and POM quality check to CI
insights.problem: CI lacked a POM quality check that can catch POM problems before publishing to Maven Central.
insights.root_cause: No step existed to run pomchecker in the existing code-quality workflow.
insights.solution: Added Set up Maven (stCarolas/setup-maven@v4.5 with maven 3.9.0) and a step to run the kordamp pomchecker plugin: 'mvn org.kordamp.maven:pomchecker-maven-plugin:1.9.0:check-maven-central -D"checker.release=false"'.
insights.lesson_learned: POM quality should be validated in CI; add explicit Maven setup in GitHub Actions when using Maven-specific plugins.

insights.title: Rename Qodana workflow to Code-Quality and add build job
insights.problem: Existing quick Qodana workflow had a single purpose; maintainers expanded it to include a build job so quality checks run alongside a full build.
insights.root_cause: Need to run code quality and build in the same workflow; previous name was 'Qodana'.
insights.solution: Rename workflow to Code-Quality and add a build job that sets up JDK, runs mvn clean verify, package and javadoc generation.
insights.lesson_learned: Naming workflows clearly (Code-Quality) and including both analysis and build steps reduces surprises in CI.

insights.title: Add Gradle Enterprise and build cache Maven extensions
insights.problem: Need to enable build scans and caching for faster CI and local builds.
insights.root_cause: Maven build didn't have enterprise/caching extensions configured.
insights.solution: Added gradle-enterprise-maven-extension and maven-build-cache-extension entries in .mvn/extensions.xml. Later some lines were removed in a subsequent commit (extension removal noted).
insights.lesson_learned: Careful with local extensions—they may affect CI reproducibility and must be committed intentionally. Keep .mvn/extensions.xml changes minimal and documented.

insights.title: Enable Gradle Enterprise and Maven Build Cache extensions
insights.problem: Local builds and CI lacked centralized build-scan / build-cache integrations that could improve build diagnostics and speed.
insights.root_cause: Maven extensions enabling Gradle Enterprise and build cache were not configured.
insights.solution: Added gradle-enterprise-maven-extension (v1.17) and maven-build-cache-extension (v1.0.0) to .mvn/extensions.xml.
insights.lesson_learned: Maven extensions in .mvn/extensions.xml can enable org-level build performance features; ensure extension files are tracked and their workspace-specific artifacts are ignored in .gitignore.

insights.title: Configure Gradle Enterprise buildScan publish mode
insights.problem: Build scan behavior needed a default mode to avoid unexpected publishing or having scans disabled.
insights.root_cause: No gradle-enterprise configuration existed to control build scan publishing.
insights.solution: Added gradle-enterprise file with <publish>ON_DEMAND</publish> to allow conditional publish of build scans.
insights.lesson_learned: When enabling build telemetry extensions, configure conservative defaults (ON_DEMAND) to avoid accidental data publishing.

insights.title: Add dedicated workflow to regenerate templated docs
insights.problem: Document/template generation needed an explicit automatic workflow for PRs to keep README/action.yml in sync.
insights.root_cause: Previously doc generation steps were embedded inconsistently in release workflows and used sed.
insights.solution: Added .github/workflows/doc.yml to run mvn resources:copy-resources on pull_request and commit changes back if any.
insights.lesson_learned: Separate doc generation into its own workflow to keep CI responsibilities focused and make it easier to reason about and test.

insights.title: Run smoke tests using the module's mvnw and set working directory for processes
insights.problem: Smoke test previously invoked ./mvnw in repo root and used relative invocations when calling Maven in cloned projects; this fails when module mvnw is inside maven_plugin submodule.
insights.root_cause: Multi-module layout introduced submodule mvnw and tests weren't adjusted to use the correct path.
insights.solution: Reference maven_plugin/./mvnw as the mavenPath, pass that to ProcBuilder and set a working directory for process execution. Use withNoTimeout and -q flags to quiet output during smoke tests.
insights.lesson_learned: When a project is multi-module and test harnesses run external processes, make sure to reference the correct wrapper path and set working directories explicitly.

insights.title: getProjectVersion must run in maven_plugin working directory
insights.problem: Determining project.version via mvn help:evaluate failed if executed outside the module directory (returned wrong project or no output).
insights.root_cause: ProcBuilder did not set workingDirectory when evaluating the version.
insights.solution: Call withWorkingDirectory(Path.of("./maven_plugin").toFile()) when executing help:evaluate.
insights.lesson_learned: External process invocations to Maven must set the module working directory to evaluate module-local properties.

insights.title: Added smoke test harness and expanded testcases
insights.problem: Need to run plugin against large real-world projects to detect integration issues.
insights.root_cause: Unit tests do not cover large-scale dependency graphs and real plugin execution behavior.
insights.solution: Added jbang-based SmokeTest.java and Git checkout logic to clone known projects, checkout specific commits, run the plugin and compare dependency-graph output to lockfile. Also added Dockerfile and CI workflow to run smoke tests.
insights.lesson_learned: Smoke tests are useful to catch platform-specific or scale issues (e.g., native classifiers, resolver differences). Ensure smoke tests pin to commits to avoid CI flakiness and use no-timeout execution for long builds.

insights.title: Smoke test improved to checkout project commit hashes and avoid timeouts
insights.problem: Earlier smoke test cloned repositories but did not fix to a commit which could produce non-reproducible results; also used ProcBuilder.run wrapper that timed out.
insights.root_cause: Unpinned repositories and default ProcBuilder usage caused non-determinism and timeouts.
insights.solution: Added CiProject record with commitHash, checkout to that commit, changed ProcBuilder usage to withNoTimeout, and refined getProjectVersion invocation.
insights.lesson_learned: Smoke tests that rely on external repositories should pin to commits and allow unbounded execution time to avoid intermittent CI failures.

insights.title: Set working-directory for smoke tests and adjust environment variables passed to jbang
insights.problem: Smoke-tests workflow assumed mvn wrapper in repo root and did not set a consistent working-directory for job steps. jbang script environment variables were mismatched.
insights.root_cause: Multi-module repository layout changes and new smoke test harness.
insights.solution: Set env working-directory: ./maven_plugin and configure defaults.run. Pass WORKING_DIRECTORY env var into jbang step.
insights.lesson_learned: CI workflows should set a working-directory when operating on submodules; keep the action and harness in sync with repository layout.
